home.hero_title: Product Designer specialized in Design Systems.

txt.001: Specialize in creating complete end-to-end solutions. Currently, I'm enhancing user experiences at The Adecco Group, thanks to an innovative design system that improves accessibility and usability.

txt.006: I am currently working at UST for The Adecco Group, an international company based in Zurich, with offices on all five continents and more than 10 brands.
txt.007: My focus is on end-to-end solutions, from generative research to tracking post-release metrics and their respective iterations, with a special focus on interaction and behavior design. I enjoy participating in research and product management.
txt.008: Having worked for various agencies, consultancies, and product companies, The Adecco Group has nurtured my professional and personal growth, with achievements such as launching a new multi-brand Design System covering the needs of five platforms, improving usability and accessibility of the platform I am responsible for by over 70%, and migrating the platform to the new TAG DS design system.
txt.009: The design process for the DS was based on Agile methodologies and data-driven decisions, considering user, business, and development cost implications.
txt.010: My current goal is to enhance my skills in accessibility and data visualization, aiming to bring valuable insights and inclusivity to our projects. Recently, I have achieved a certification in accessibility, underscoring my dedication to this area. My long-term focus is on improving internal design team processes to be more efficient, increase my seniority, and enhance my craft.
txt.013: I love nature and dedicate all my free time to activities that involve being in touch with it. Surfing 🏄🏻 revitalizes me with the energy of the sea, while being in the mountains 🏔️ allows me to discover places that no one has ever set foot on before. I also enjoy traveling in my van. But without a doubt, one of the things I enjoy most is exploring and tasting the local flavors 🍕 of each destination. These experiences are not just hobbies, but a lifestyle that nourishes my physical and mental well-being, bringing balance and happiness to my life.

txt.019: Adecco Connect is a platform that centralizes the selection processes and job posting for recruiters working at Adecco.
txt.022: My role was pivotal in bridging the gap between client requirements and effective design solutions. I played a key part in gathering specific needs from various countries using Adecco Connect. Through collaboration with development and business teams, I translated these requirements into actionable design solutions.
txt.023: The goal was to enhance brand consistency and user trust by ensuring a unified and recognizable design language across platforms. Secondly, the system significantly sped up the development process, allowing for quicker responses to market demands and efficient feature rollouts. Thirdly, the system's scalability facilitated the company's digital expansion, ensuring adaptability to new platforms and technologies.
txt.025: Adecco Connect was facing several issues: they were not using a design system, there were numerous usability issues and errors, the color red was being used incorrectly, and it was not responsive. To improve the product, I had to identify inconsistencies using Nielsen's heuristics and usability and accessibility rules to present the issues to various stakeholders.
txt.027: As a Product Designer , I played a crucial role in bridging the gap between client requirements and effective design solutions, translating needs into actionable design solutions.
txt.030: The process began with an exhaustive phase of research and analysis, where interviews with stakeholders and analysis of competitor design systems were conducted to understand their needs and pain points.
txt.032: Design principles focused on accessibility, reusability, and simplicity were established. These principles were crucial in the creation of all components, including the Time Picker, ensuring they were intuitive and accessible to all users.
txt.034: Figma was used for design collaboration and prototyping, Zeroheight for component documentation, and Storybook for documenting and sharing system components with developers.
txt.036: Your browser doesn't support HTML5 video. Download the video instead.
txt.038: Video demonstration of the redesigned Adecco Connect platform interface
txt.039: The solution involved integrating a robust design system aligned with established usability and accessibility standards. Additionally, a redesigned user interface (UI) focused on clarity and engagement was introduced. This holistic approach ensured consistency, intuitive interactions, and inclusivity.
txt.041: Participant segmentation involved 16 usability testing sessions with Connect users, exploring the workflow to create a recruitment process. The features investigated included:
txt.042: Setting up a new recruitment process
txt.044: Adding a hard skill test to the process
txt.046: The key points of the research included evaluating the overall experience of Connect and measuring the usability and satisfaction of recruiters with the new feature. The study aimed to uncover usability issues faced by real users, highlighting:
txt.047: The error rate for mistaken clicks in the drag-and-drop video functionality was 50%, indicating that the feature was not perceived as directly visible to participants
txt.048: Even though participants rated the task as easy, it took them 3 clicks to achieve the task's objective
txt.049: Usability Measurement Score of TAG DS
txt.050: Usability Measurement Score of TAG DS
txt.052: The solution, informed by user testing feedback, focused on better hierarchy in typography use, improved visual separation of sections, and more optimal use of spacing. Typography hierarchy is crucial for clear communication and readability, helping to organize information so readers can easily grasp the content structure and focus on key points.
txt.053: Usability Measurement Score of TAG DS
txt.054: Usability Measurement Score of TAG DS
txt.056: The process highlighted the significance of interdisciplinary collaboration and user feedback in the development of design systems. Clear and accessible documentation is key to the system's adoption and maintenance.
txt.058: In conclusion, the development and implementation of the TAG DS Design System in Adecco Connect marked a transformative journey, featuring challenges, learnings, and notably, significant strides towards achieving the vision of delivering exceptional user experiences. This project not only set a new standard in design and development within The Adecco Group but also reaffirmed the commitment to innovation, quality, and user satisfaction. With this solid foundation, there's enthusiastic anticipation for the future chapters in the digital transformation story.

txt.017: Redesigning Guuk's commercial site: Enhancing navigation and customer service in the telecommunications sector
txt.020: Guuk, part of the Más Móvil telecommunications group, faced the challenge of redesigning its commercial website to align with its new brand image and enhance customer engagement and experience. My role in leading the redesign involved revamping the user interface and website experience, focusing on creating visually appealing designs, intuitive navigation, and smooth interactions for visitors. Understanding Guuk's brand identity and target audience, I designed compelling layouts, graphics, and interactive elements to boost user engagement and satisfaction.
txt.022: Guuk's customer service team was receiving a high volume of calls from customers for basic issues and needed a secondary channel on the website to address inquiries. This would relieve the only available channel at that time, which was the telephone channel.
txt.023: The goal was to create a new section within the website, featuring a well-structured information architecture, enabling customers to find answers to their questions as quickly as possible.
txt.026: The first step involved gathering all the common questions to optimize space on the website, enabling customers to quickly find solutions to their problems. A decision was made to implement a dynamic search engine, including a search box in a Frequently Asked Questions (FAQ) section, thereby enhancing the user experience. This allowed visitors to swiftly find specific information tailored to their needs.
txt.029: By incorporating a search engine into the FAQs, we made it easier for users to save time, improve their navigation, and easily access relevant answers without scrolling through a long list of questions and answers. This solution not only enhanced user autonomy in resolving queries but also helped reduce the load on the telephone customer service.
txt.031: The launch of the new FAQ section with dynamic search significantly improved customers' ability to find solutions on their own, quickly. This enhancement led to a notable increase in customer satisfaction, evidenced by fewer support calls for basic inquiries, thus freeing up resources for more complex questions. Moreover, this user-centric approach has strengthened Guuk's brand image as one that values and facilitates its customers' experience.
txt.033: This project emphasized the importance of deeply understanding user needs and behaviors to design effective solutions that not only address immediate challenges but also contribute to the long-term goals of the company. Collaboration between teams and integrating user feedback were key to the project's success.
txt.035: The redesign of Guuk's commercial website showcases the transformative impact of a systematic and collaborative design approach on user experience and brand-customer relationship. By enhancing site navigation and information access through a dynamic FAQ section, Guuk has set a new customer service standard in the telecommunications sector, emphasizing its commitment to innovation and customer satisfaction. This project not only improved user experience but also marked a significant shift in Guuk's approach to digital solution design and development.

txt.017: Improving live monitoring, accessibility, and incident review workflows for a proctoring SaaS
txt.020: Smowltech provides online proctoring solutions used by universities and training platforms. The product includes live monitoring, technical status signals (camera, audio, lock tools), and a review flow for incidents generated during exams. My role focused on improving usability and accessibility across key workflows, while scaling UI consistency through a shared design system.
txt.022: Operators needed to make fast decisions under pressure, but the UI contained dense information, inconsistent patterns, and accessibility gaps. This increased cognitive load during live exams and slowed down incident review afterwards.
txt.023: The goal was to make critical monitoring signals easier to scan, reduce errors, and ensure core flows were accessible (keyboard, focus visibility, contrast, and assistive technology friendly) without slowing the product down.
txt.026: I worked with product and engineering to map the most critical moments during an exam (live monitoring) and the highest-friction steps after an exam (incident review). We audited current UI patterns, gathered operator pain points, and validated requirements against accessibility best practices (keyboard navigation, focus management, contrast, and clear status messaging).
txt.029: I introduced clearer information hierarchy for live monitoring (what matters now vs. what can wait), standardized components through the design system, and strengthened accessibility basics (visible focus, consistent labels, predictable keyboard order, and readable status messaging). For incident review, the goal was to make evidence easier to interpret and quicker to act on.
txt.031: The improvements reduced cognitive load for operators, made core flows more consistent, and increased accessibility coverage across key screens. Internally, shared components and patterns improved design-to-dev handoff and helped teams iterate faster without re-solving the same UI problems.
txt.033: Operational products need ruthless prioritization of “what matters right now.” Accessibility and usability become dramatically easier to maintain when patterns are standardized and validated early (instead of fixed later). Tight collaboration with engineering was essential to keep changes safe and incremental.
txt.035: This case study highlights how improving hierarchy, consistency, and accessibility can make high-pressure workflows safer and faster. By strengthening the foundations (design system + accessibility practices) and targeting the most critical moments (live monitoring + incident review), Smowltech can scale features while keeping the experience usable for everyone.

txt.017: The Adecco Group's multi-brand design system encompasses all digital platforms within the group.
txt.020: At the core of The Adecco Group's digital transformation was the recognition of the need for a unified Design System, named TAG DS. This system aimed to standardize UI/UX components across all digital platforms, enhancing visual cohesion, usability, and accessibility. It facilitated the development of digital products for the group's various brands, such as Adecco, Akkodis, and LHH, streamlining processes and ensuring a consistent user experience across the brand portfolio.
txt.022: One major challenge was the diversity of existing user interfaces, developed in isolation by different teams, leading to a lack of coherence and variable accessibility standards. Conducting a comprehensive audit of the existing platforms was particularly challenging due to their disparate construction. This variability posed a significant obstacle in understanding the current state and planning for the cohesive integration of the Design System.
txt.024: As a Product Designer and Design System Contributor , my role was to coordinate the collaborative effort among designers, developers, and stakeholders to lay the groundwork for the design system, from the Discovery phase through to the final Construct phase. This involved documentation and publication on Zeroheight, ensuring the system met both design and business objectives.
txt.027: The process began with an exhaustive research and analysis phase, including interviews with stakeholders, analysis of competitors' design systems, and workshops with User Personas defined by the Research team. This was aimed at understanding their needs and pain points.
txt.029: Principles of design centered on accessibility, reusability, and simplicity were established. These principles were crucial in the creation of all components, including the Time Picker, ensuring they were intuitive and accessible to all users.
txt.031: Figma was used for design and prototyping collaboration, Zeroheight for component documentation, and Storybook for documenting and sharing system components with developers.
txt.034: The Time Picker was designed to address the need for efficient and accessible time input in time management and scheduling applications. The lack of consistency in previous time input solutions had resulted in a fragmented user experience.
txt.036: The design of the Time Picker started with initial sketches and wireframes, followed by interactive prototypes in Figma. Usability testing was conducted to gather feedback, leading to several design iterations to improve ease of use and accessibility. Close collaboration with developers ensured effective technical implementation consistent with the design.
txt.038: The component was rigorously tested to ensure its compatibility with WCAG accessibility standards and its smooth integration with other components of the design system, maintaining visual and functional cohesion.
txt.043: Since its launch, the TAG DS has been widely adopted by product teams, with the Time Picker receiving praise for its ease of use and accessibility. The design system has facilitated UI consistency across platforms and accelerated the development of new products. The Usability Measurement Score has increased from 45% with the first version of the TAG DS V.0 design system to 72% with the latest version, TAG DS V.1.
txt.044: Usability Measurement Score of TAG DS
txt.045: Usability Measurement Score of TAG DS
txt.047: The process underscored the importance of interdisciplinary collaboration and user feedback in the development of design systems. It was also learned that clear and accessible documentation is key to the adoption and maintenance of the system.
txt.048: The Future of the Design System
txt.049: Based on continuous feedback, the design system is constantly evolving, with plans to expand its component library and improve design guidelines to adapt to new technologies and user needs.
txt.051: The creation of The Adecco Group's Design System has been a journey of collaboration, innovation, and learning. Through the creation of components such as the Time Picker, we have established a new standard of design and development within the company, demonstrating the power of a well-executed design system to transform the user experience and facilitate product development.
